observed_facts: Terminal event bonuses (r_safe_contact_once, r_stable_landing_once) paid 0 times in any episode
Dominant component r_approach_region (ratio 0.451), always triggered, mean 1.328/step
r_stability and r_landing_quality also triggered every step, significant magnitude
Repeated leg‑contact toggles (max 22 toggles for left_contact) indicating unstable bouncing
Hack flags: repeated_event_exploitation = true, shaping_goal_mismatch = true
Episode length mean = 692.5 steps, suggesting long episodes ending in crash
likely_true_failures: Policy exploits dense shaping rewards to hover/bounce near the landing pad instead of landing
Terminal event bonuses are too difficult to achieve or too weak to overcome the per‑step reward attraction
r_approach_region alone heavily dominates the reward signal, leading to local optima of endless hovering
root_cause_hypotheses: Continuous shaping components (especially r_approach_region) are overweighted relative to terminal success rewards, creating a per‑step incentive to remain in a ‘good’ region without completing the task
The one‑time event conditions (duration 2 and 5) are too strict for the current policy to ever satisfy, preventing any terminal reward from being discovered
Lack of any penalty for time or crashing allows the policy to accrue large shaping rewards indefinitely

avoid_actions: Do not reduce only r_approach_region weight in isolation – exploitation may shift to r_stability or r_landing_quality
Avoid raising terminal bonus magnitudes without also adjusting shaping components, as that may lead to equally unwanted trade‑offs
recommended_actions: Apply a coupled rebalancing that significantly reduces the scale of all continuous shaping components (r_approach_region, r_stability, r_landing_quality) while making terminal event bonuses more attainable (e.g., relax duration or increase bonus)
Consider adding a soft time or crash penalty to discourage indefinite episodes

reward_editor.rationale: Following reflection strategy: cut continuous shaping weights by ~65% across all three major dense components, tighten the clip on r_approach_region to cap per‑step reward, and relax duration conditions on both one‑time terminal bonuses to make them achievable. This atomic package avoids solo changes that could shift exploitation and preserves a minimal shaping gradient while clearly making landing the dominant reward signal.
reward_editor.expected_effect: Shaping rewards become too weak to justify prolonged hovering; the policy will be forced to attempt landing earlier, increasing the probability of triggering terminal bonuses. Success rate should rise and episode lengths should decrease, while still retaining enough gradient for initial learning.
reward_editor.risk_analysis: Aggressive reduction (65%) could make overall rewards too sparse and cause performance collapse. The relaxed duration of safe_contact to 1 step might accidentally reward a brief, unstable touch that is immediately followed by a crash. Strict monitoring of success rate and mean episode length after the edit is essential.

avoid_next: Solo decrease_weight on a single shaping component
Increasing terminal bonus magnitude without simultaneous shaping reduction
recommend_next: Atomic package that jointly cuts dense shaping and improves terminal bonus attainability
Use of clip constraints on dominant components to cap per‑step maxima
applicability_notes: This environment requires dominant shaping to be tempered to prevent hovering exploitation
One‑time event rules should have durations relaxed only to the point of safe contact / stable landing, not to trivial contacts

diagnosis: High rewards but zero landings: dense shaping components (r_approach_region dominant) incentivize indefinite hovering and contact toggling, while terminal one‑time bonuses never trigger due to strict duration requirements. An atomic rebalancing sharply reduces all continuous shaping and relaxes terminal event durations to shift incentive toward successful landing.
